keyword: High Visibility Safety Apparel - CSA Standard Z96-22
title: High Visibility Safety Apparel - CSA Standard Z96-22
answer: This knowledge entry explains the difference between CSA visibility classes and levels, common online store confusion, approved colours, and high-risk compliance expectations for roadside work.
raw_text:
META_ID: SBA-KB-0001
SOURCE_ID: SB-2025-PPE-4
TITLE: High Visibility Safety Apparel - CSA Standard Z96-22
CATEGORY: PPE
SUBCATEGORY: High Visibility Clothing
TOPICS: high visibility, CSA Z96-22, PPE, apparel classes, apparel levels, towing safety, roadside safety, CCOHS, fluorescent colours, reflective trim
SEARCH_TAGS: hi vis, high vis, visibility apparel, CSA vest, Class 1, Class 2, Class 3, Level 1, Level 2, FR, fire resistant, towing PPE, roadside worker clothing
REGION: Canada
INDUSTRY: Towing, Roadside Assistance, Emergency Response, Traffic Control
SOURCE_TYPE: Safety Buzz Alert
LANGUAGE: English
RELIABILITY: Field-use training summary
LAST_REVIEWED: 2026-03-26

SUMMARY:
This knowledge entry explains the difference between CSA visibility classes and levels, common online store confusion, approved colours, and high-risk compliance expectations for roadside work.

KEY_KNOWLEDGE:
1. CSA high-visibility apparel uses both CLASS and LEVEL ratings.
2. LEVEL refers to retroreflective performance.
3. Level 1 means lower to moderate retroreflective performance.
4. Level 2 means higher and stronger reflectivity in low-light conditions.
5. CLASS refers to job risk level and body coverage requirements.
6. Class 1 is low risk.
7. Class 2 is medium risk.
8. Class 3 is high risk.
9. FR means fire resistant.

IMPORTANT_DISTINCTION:
Many workers confuse Class and Level.
Class and Level are not the same thing.
A garment can be Class 2 Level 2.
The class tells you the risk/application category.
The level tells you the reflectivity performance.

FIELD_NOTE_01:
Two garments may both be Class 2 Level 2 even if one has extra orange striping.
That extra orange strip may be called visibility-enhancing trim (VE trim).
VE trim can be permitted as an added safety feature.
Extra trim does not automatically change the class by itself.

FIELD_NOTE_02:
Online product descriptions can be misleading.
A seller may say “meets CSA standards” without clearly stating class, level, fabric type, or approved fluorescent background colour.
Do not assume every reflective shirt or hoodie is Class 2 Level 2.

FIELD_NOTE_03:
A dark or non-fluorescent garment with reflective striping may not qualify as Class 2 Level 2.
If the background material is not an approved fluorescent colour, it may not meet the higher visibility class expected for roadside towing work.

APPROVED_BACKGROUND_COLOURS:
Approved fluorescent background colours shown in this training summary:
- Fluorescent yellow-green
- Fluorescent orange-red

CAUTION_ON_COLOURS:
Only approved fluorescent colours should be relied on for CSA Class 2 or Class 3 visibility expectations.
Non-fluorescent colours can create confusion and may not meet the intended visibility class.

FABRIC_AND_MATERIAL_NOTE:
This training summary states that 100 percent cotton clothing will not be Level 2 Class 2 in the example shown.
Polyester-based reflective and visibility materials are generally used where stronger strip performance and fade resistance are needed.

REFLECTIVE_TRIM_NOTE:
The reflected strips shown in the training summary are listed as 50 mm (2 inches) wide.

“X” PATTERN NOTE:
Approved CSA garments commonly show the high-visibility striping pattern clearly.
This training summary notes that approved Z96 garments will have the visible “X” style pattern shown in the example.

CCOHS_COMPLIANCE_GUIDANCE:
For high-risk work:
- Class 2 may be acceptable for daytime
- Class 3 is expected for low-light conditions

HIGH_RISK_WORK_FACTORS:
High-risk visibility requirements become more important when:
- Vehicles are exceeding 80 km/h (50 mph)
- Vehicle operators have high task loads
- Workers must be visible through a full range of body motions
- Work is taking place in low light or at night

JOBS_LISTED_AS_EXAMPLES:
Examples named in this training summary:
- Towing operators
- Emergency responders
- Road assistance workers
- Flagging crews

TARA_RESPONSE_GUIDANCE:
If asked what towing workers should wear on high-speed roads, TARA should explain:
1. The worker must verify the garment class and level, not just “reflective.”
2. Class and Level mean different things.
3. Approved fluorescent colours matter.
4. For high-risk roadside work, daytime and low-light needs may differ.
5. Night or low-light operations may require Class 3 visibility protection.
6. Do not trust online descriptions unless the class, level, and approved colour are clearly identified.

COMMON_USER_QUESTION:
Q: What is the difference between Class 2 and Level 2?
A: Class 2 refers to the job risk/application category. Level 2 refers to stronger retroreflective performance in low-light conditions.

COMMON_USER_QUESTION:
Q: If a shirt has reflective stripes, does that mean it is CSA Class 2 Level 2?
A: No. Reflective stripes alone do not confirm the garment class and level. Background colour, material, striping design, and the garment certification details matter.

COMMON_USER_QUESTION:
Q: Can orange trim be added and still be compliant?
A: Yes, visibility-enhancing trim may be permitted as an additional safety feature, but it does not replace the need to meet the proper class and level requirements.

COMMON_USER_QUESTION:
Q: Is black high-visibility clothing always CSA compliant for roadside towing?
A: No. Dark garments can create confusion. If the background material is not an approved fluorescent colour, it may not meet the visibility class expected for higher-risk roadside work.

KEYWORDS_FOR_TARA_MATCHING:
high visibility apparel
hi vis clothing
CSA Z96
CSA Z96-22
Class 1
Class 2
Class 3
Level 1
Level 2
FR clothing
fire resistant high vis
visibility enhancing trim
VE trim
approved fluorescent colours
fluorescent yellow green
fluorescent orange red
roadside worker PPE
tow operator vest
night towing PPE
80 km/h visibility requirement

SAFETY_MESSAGE:
For towing and roadside operations, never buy PPE based only on appearance or online marketing language. Verify the class, verify the level, verify the fluorescent background colour, and match the garment to the roadway risk and lighting conditions.

keyword: Why Visibility Level Matters – Class 3 vs Class 1 for Roadside Work
title: Why Visibility Level Matters – Class 3 vs Class 1 for Roadside Work
answer: This knowledge highlights the critical difference in visibility between CSA Class 3 and lower-class garments. It reinforces that being “seen” is not guaranteed unless the correct class of PPE is used based on roadway speed and lighting conditions.
raw_text:
META_ID: SBA-KB-0003
SOURCE_ID: SB-2026-VIS-03
TITLE: Why Visibility Level Matters – Class 3 vs Class 1 for Roadside Work
CATEGORY: PPE
SUBCATEGORY: High Visibility Decision Making
TOPICS: visibility comparison, Class 3 vs Class 1, roadside exposure, daytime vs nighttime safety, CCOHS guidance, towing PPE, client safety
SEARCH_TAGS: Class 3 vs Class 1, visibility difference, who can see you, towing visibility, roadside PPE Canada, high vis comparison, daytime vs night visibility
REGION: Canada
INDUSTRY: Towing, Roadside Assistance, Emergency Response
SOURCE_TYPE: Safety Buzz Alert
LANGUAGE: English
RELIABILITY: Field-based + CCOHS guidance
LAST_REVIEWED: 2026-03-26

SUMMARY:
This knowledge highlights the critical difference in visibility between CSA Class 3 and lower-class garments. It reinforces that being “seen” is not guaranteed unless the correct class of PPE is used based on roadway speed and lighting conditions.

CORE_MESSAGE:
The goal of high-visibility PPE is not just compliance — it is to be seen early enough for drivers to react.

RIGHT_VS_WRONG:
RIGHT:
- CSA Class 3 garments provide maximum visibility
- Full body coverage improves recognition of a human figure
- More effective in both day and night conditions

WRONG:
- Class 1 Level 2 garments provide insufficient visibility for roadside towing work
- Limited coverage reduces recognition distance
- Workers may not be seen early enough by drivers

CRITICAL_VISIBILITY_CONCEPT:
Seeing a worker late is the same as not seeing them at all.

CCOHS_GUIDANCE:
According to Canadian Centre for Occupational Health and Safety (CCOHS):
- Workers exposed to traffic over 80 km/h:
  - Minimum: Class 2 Level 2 in perfect daylight
  - Recommended: Class 3 for poor daylight or nighttime conditions

DAY_VS_NIGHT_VISIBILITY:
- Daytime visibility still requires fluorescent contrast
- Nighttime visibility relies heavily on reflective striping
- Class 3 significantly improves recognition in both conditions

REAL_WORLD_VISIBILITY_FAILURE:
Workers wearing lower-class garments may:
- Blend into background environments
- Be detected too late by approaching drivers
- Appear as objects instead of human figures

CLIENT_RISK_FACTOR:
Not only operators are at risk:
- Customers/clients standing near the roadside
- Often have no PPE
- Are even less visible than workers

KEY_QUESTION:
“Who actually sees your client?”
This highlights that:
- Visibility planning must include everyone on scene
- Not just the operator wearing PPE

HIGH_RISK_SCENARIOS:
- High-speed highways (>80 km/h)
- Nighttime or low-light conditions
- Poor weather or reduced contrast environments
- Curved roads or limited sightlines

TARA_RESPONSE_GUIDANCE:
If asked what visibility level is required for towing operations, TARA should explain:
1. Class 3 provides the highest visibility and recognition.
2. Class 1 is not appropriate for roadside towing exposure.
3. Class 2 may be minimum for daytime, but Class 3 is strongly recommended.
4. Nighttime work significantly increases risk.
5. Visibility must include both worker and client positioning.

COMMON_USER_QUESTION:
Q: Is Class 1 Level 2 good enough for roadside towing?
A: No. It does not provide sufficient visibility for high-speed roadside work. Class 3 is recommended, especially in low light or night conditions.

COMMON_USER_QUESTION:
Q: Why is Class 3 better than Class 2 or Class 1?
A: Class 3 provides greater body coverage and improves the driver’s ability to recognize a human figure earlier, increasing reaction time.

COMMON_USER_QUESTION:
Q: Does visibility matter in daytime?
A: Yes. Fluorescent materials improve contrast in daylight, and higher-class garments still improve early detection.

COMMON_USER_QUESTION:
Q: Who is most at risk on a roadside scene?
A: Both the operator and the client. Clients are often not wearing PPE and may be harder to see.

KEYWORDS_FOR_TARA_MATCHING:
Class 3 visibility
Class 1 vs Class 3
who can see you roadside
towing PPE requirement Canada
CCOHS visibility rules
daytime vs nighttime visibility
client roadside safety
highway visibility risk
tow operator seen distance

SAFETY_MESSAGE:
Being visible is not about wearing reflective clothing — it is about being seen early enough for drivers to react. Class 3 visibility significantly increases your chance of being recognized as a person, not an object.

keyword: Set, Plant, Secure – Preventing Secondary Impact and Runaway Truck Risk
title: Set, Plant, Secure – Preventing Secondary Impact and Runaway Truck Risk
answer: Proper truck positioning, wheel direction, and brake application are critical to reducing the risk of a tow truck being pushed into the operator during a collision. Safety begins before exiting the vehicle.
raw_text:
META_ID: SBA-KB-0004
SOURCE_ID: SB-2025-TOW-03
TITLE: Set, Plant, Secure – Preventing Secondary Impact and Runaway Truck Risk
CATEGORY: Roadside Operations
SUBCATEGORY: Vehicle Positioning and Scene Setup
TOPICS: emergency brake, wheel positioning, truck angle, roadside setup, secondary collision risk, towing safety checklist
SEARCH_TAGS: parking brake towing, wheel angle safety, roadside positioning, tow truck setup, prevent truck movement, secondary impact towing, bowling pin effect
REGION: Canada / North America
INDUSTRY: Towing, Roadside Assistance
SOURCE_TYPE: Safety Buzz Alert
LANGUAGE: English
RELIABILITY: Field-tested operational safety
LAST_REVIEWED: 2026-03-26

SUMMARY:
Proper truck positioning, wheel direction, and brake application are critical to reducing the risk of a tow truck being pushed into the operator during a collision. Safety begins before exiting the vehicle.

CORE_MESSAGE:
Roadside safety starts before you step out of the truck.

SET_PLANT_SECURE_MODEL:
SET:
- Assess the breakdown location
- Activate emergency flashers and 4-way lights

PLANT:
- Position truck a safe distance from the stranded vehicle
- Angle the truck toward the ditch or shoulder
- Turn wheels hard toward the ditch (or safe direction)
- If possible, keep at least two wheels on pavement for stability

SECURE:
- Apply the emergency/parking brake
- Do not rely on transmission “Park” alone
- Confirm vehicle stability before exiting

CRITICAL_WARNING:
Do not trust the transmission park position (parking pawl) alone.
Without the parking brake applied, a collision can push the truck a significant distance.

REAL_WORLD_RISK:
If struck:
- A service truck can be pushed at significant speed (example shown: ~48.9 km/h after impact)
- This can carry the truck directly into the operator or work zone

PHYSICS_CONCEPT_SIMPLIFIED:
- A moving vehicle striking a parked tow truck transfers energy
- If the truck is not properly secured, it will travel in the direction of impact
- Wheel angle and brake force reduce travel distance and redirect movement

EXPECTED_BEHAVIOR_WITH_PROPER_SETUP:
- Parking brake reduces travel distance
- Wheel angle redirects truck away from operator
- Truck may still move, but with reduced severity and safer direction

BOWLING_PIN_EFFECT:
If improperly positioned:
- The tow truck can be driven forward into the operator
- Similar to a “bowling pin” effect
- This is one of the most dangerous roadside scenarios

CHECKLIST_FOR_OPERATORS:
1. Assess the breakdown location
2. Use emergency flashers and lighting
3. Park truck away from the vehicle and angle it properly
4. Turn wheels hard toward safe direction
5. Apply emergency/parking brake
6. Put on PPE before exiting
7. Set pylons (approx. 5 meters apart downstream)
8. Keep client in their vehicle when possible
9. Maintain awareness of oncoming traffic at all times

CLIENT_SAFETY_NOTE:
Clients should remain inside their vehicle whenever possible, as it is typically safer than standing roadside.

TARA_RESPONSE_GUIDANCE:
If asked how to safely position a tow truck, TARA should explain:
1. Never rely on “Park” alone — always apply the parking brake
2. Angle the truck away from the work area
3. Turn wheels to redirect movement if struck
4. Create a buffer zone using the truck
5. Always assume a secondary impact can occur

COMMON_USER_QUESTION:
Q: Is putting the truck in park enough when working roadside?
A: No. The parking brake must be applied. A collision can push a truck in park a significant distance.

COMMON_USER_QUESTION:
Q: Why turn the wheels when parking roadside?
A: Turning the wheels helps redirect the truck away from the operator if it is struck by another vehicle.

COMMON_USER_QUESTION:
Q: What is the biggest risk when setting up on the highway?
A: Being struck by a vehicle and having the tow truck pushed into the operator (secondary impact).

COMMON_USER_QUESTION:
Q: Where should the client be during a roadside call?
A: Inside their vehicle whenever possible, as it is generally safer than standing outside.

KEYWORDS_FOR_TARA_MATCHING:
parking brake towing safety
wheel angle roadside
tow truck positioning
secondary impact risk
bowling pin effect towing
roadside setup checklist
tow operator safety steps
prevent truck movement collision
highway towing safety setup

SAFETY_MESSAGE:
Set. Plant. Secure. Your truck is your first line of protection. If it is not properly positioned and secured, it can become the hazard.

keyword: Spare Tire Inflation Hazard – Losing Your Safe Work Zone
title: Spare Tire Inflation Hazard – Losing Your Safe Work Zone
answer: A common but overlooked hazard occurs when a spare tire is installed without checking air pressure. This forces the operator to reposition the truck after the job, eliminating the original protected work zone and increasing exposure to traffic.
raw_text:
META_ID: SBA-KB-0005
SOURCE_ID: SB-2025-TOW-05
TITLE: Spare Tire Inflation Hazard – Losing Your Safe Work Zone
CATEGORY: Roadside Operations
SUBCATEGORY: Post-Service Risk / Tire Service
TOPICS: tire change safety, spare tire PSI, repositioning risk, work zone loss, roadside exposure, tow operator positioning
SEARCH_TAGS: spare tire PSI, roadside tire inflation, tow operator risk after job, losing work zone safety, tire change hazard, repositioning danger towing
REGION: Canada / North America
INDUSTRY: Towing, Roadside Assistance
SOURCE_TYPE: Safety Buzz Alert
LANGUAGE: English
RELIABILITY: Field-observed hazard
LAST_REVIEWED: 2026-03-26

SUMMARY:
A common but overlooked hazard occurs when a spare tire is installed without checking air pressure. This forces the operator to reposition the truck after the job, eliminating the original protected work zone and increasing exposure to traffic.

CORE_MESSAGE:
A job is not safe just because it is “almost done.”

THE FORGOTTEN STEP:
Failing to check spare tire PSI before installation.

HOW_THE_RISK_DEVELOPS:
1. Operator arrives and sets up a safe work zone properly
2. Tire change is completed
3. Spare tire is flat or underinflated
4. Air line cannot reach from the current safe position
5. Operator repositions the truck in front of the vehicle
6. Original safety zone is lost

CRITICAL_HAZARD:
- Safety lights become less effective to oncoming traffic
- Drivers have reduced time to react or move over
- Traffic flow becomes unpredictable
- Operator is exposed in a more dangerous position

REAL_WORLD_CONSEQUENCE:
Even when traffic slows down:
- It is still unsafe due to:
  - Limited reaction time
  - Vehicle mechanical limitations
  - Driver behavior inconsistencies

LOSS_OF_PROTECTION_CONCEPT:
Your original setup created a protected work area.
Repositioning the truck removes that protection.

HIGH_RISK_SCENARIOS:
- High-speed roads
- Limited shoulder space
- Night or reduced visibility
- Heavy traffic flow

BEST_PRACTICES:
1. Always check PSI in the spare tire BEFORE installing it
2. Inflate the spare tire before mounting if needed
3. Avoid repositioning the truck after setup whenever possible
4. If inflation is required after installation:
   - Perform it behind the truck (safer zone)
   - Maintain visual awareness of oncoming traffic

SAFE_POSITIONING_RULE:
Work toward the rear of the service truck whenever possible to maintain a buffer zone.

TARA_RESPONSE_GUIDANCE:
If asked about tire change safety, TARA should explain:
1. Always verify spare tire pressure before installation
2. Avoid breaking your established safety zone
3. Repositioning increases exposure risk significantly
4. The safest work area is typically behind the service truck
5. Always monitor traffic, even after the job appears complete

COMMON_USER_QUESTION:
Q: Why should I check the spare tire pressure before installing it?
A: If the spare is flat, you may need to reposition your truck after installation, which removes your safe work zone and increases risk.

COMMON_USER_QUESTION:
Q: What is the danger of moving the truck after a tire change?
A: You lose your protected work zone, making your safety lights less effective and increasing exposure to traffic.

COMMON_USER_QUESTION:
Q: Where is the safest place to inflate a tire roadside?
A: Behind the service truck, where you maintain a buffer from oncoming traffic.

COMMON_USER_QUESTION:
Q: When is the highest risk during a tire change?
A: Often after the job seems complete, when safety steps are skipped or the work zone is changed.

KEYWORDS_FOR_TARA_MATCHING:
spare tire PSI towing
tire change roadside risk
reposition truck danger
lose work zone safety
tow operator tire inflation
post job hazard towing
roadside air tire safety
safe zone towing setup

SAFETY_MESSAGE:
The most dangerous moment can be after the job feels finished. Protect your work zone from start to finish — don’t install trouble.

raw_text:
META_ID: SBA-KB-0005D

keyword: Lane-Keeping Systems Interfering with Move Over Law
title: Lane-Keeping Systems Interfering with Move Over Law
answer: Lane-keeping assist systems can interfere with a driver’s attempt to move over for roadside workers. These systems may resist steering input or steer the vehicle back toward the lane, increasing the risk to tow operators and emergency responders.
raw_text:
META_ID: SBA-KB-0002
SOURCE_ID: SB-2025-VIS-02
TITLE: Lane-Keeping Systems Interfering with Move Over Law
CATEGORY: Roadside Safety
SUBCATEGORY: Vehicle Technology Risk
TOPICS: lane-keeping assist, move over law, driver behavior, roadside hazards, ADAS systems, towing safety, emergency response risk
SEARCH_TAGS: lane keeping assist, LKA, move over law, roadside danger, tow operator safety, ADAS risk, driver assist systems, highway shoulder danger
REGION: Canada / North America
INDUSTRY: Towing, Emergency Response, Roadside Assistance
SOURCE_TYPE: Safety Buzz Alert
LANGUAGE: English
RELIABILITY: Field-observed hazard
LAST_REVIEWED: 2026-03-26

SUMMARY:
Lane-keeping assist systems can interfere with a driver’s attempt to move over for roadside workers. These systems may resist steering input or steer the vehicle back toward the lane, increasing the risk to tow operators and emergency responders.

CORE_CONCEPT:
New vehicle technology can introduce unintended safety risks in real-world roadside environments.

HOW_THE_RISK_OCCURS:
1. Lane-keeping systems are designed to keep vehicles centered between lane markings.
2. When a vehicle approaches roadside workers on the shoulder, the system may interpret the lane line as a hard boundary.
3. If a driver attempts to move over:
   - The system may resist the steering input
   - The system may attempt to steer the vehicle back into the lane
4. If the driver does not apply strong steering input or use the turn signal properly:
   - The vehicle may drift back toward the shoulder
   - This creates a direct hazard to roadside workers

CRITICAL_BEHAVIOR_PATTERN:
Driver tries to move over → Lane-keeping system fights back → Vehicle returns toward workers

SAFETY_IMPLICATIONS:
- Delayed driver reaction when moving over
- Erratic or unpredictable vehicle movement
- Reduced effectiveness of Move Over laws
- Increased risk of near-miss or struck-by incidents

IMPORTANT_REALITY:
This risk is:
- Not yet fully reflected in formal regulations or advisories
- Already present in real-world roadside environments
- Increasing as more vehicles use driver-assist systems

HIGH_RISK_SCENARIOS:
- High-speed highways
- Narrow shoulders
- Limited escape routes for operators
- Night or low-visibility conditions
- Drivers unfamiliar with their vehicle technology

WHO_IS_AT_RISK:
- Tow truck operators
- Emergency responders
- Roadside assistance workers
- Traffic control personnel

TARA_RESPONSE_GUIDANCE:
If a user asks about roadside safety risks from modern vehicles, TARA should explain:
1. Driver-assist systems like lane-keeping can override or resist driver intent.
2. Moving over may not happen smoothly or immediately.
3. Operators should never assume a vehicle will move over safely.
4. Always maintain escape routes and situational awareness.
5. Expect unpredictable vehicle behavior near the shoulder.

COMMON_USER_QUESTION:
Q: Can lane-keeping assist actually steer a vehicle back toward a tow operator?
A: Yes. If the system detects lane markings as boundaries, it may resist steering or guide the vehicle back into the lane, potentially toward the roadside worker.

COMMON_USER_QUESTION:
Q: Why didn’t the driver move over even though they tried?
A: The lane-keeping system may have interfered by resisting the steering input or attempting to keep the vehicle centered.

COMMON_USER_QUESTION:
Q: Is this risk recognized in regulations?
A: Not fully. This is an emerging real-world hazard that is not yet clearly reflected in many formal safety advisories.

KEYWORDS_FOR_TARA_MATCHING:
lane keeping assist risk
ADAS roadside danger
move over law failure
driver assist interference
tow operator struck risk
vehicle steering override
lane assist hazard towing
highway shoulder danger
roadside worker risk modern vehicles

SAFETY_MESSAGE:
Never assume a driver will successfully move over. Modern driver-assist systems can delay, resist, or override steering inputs. Always work as if the vehicle may come toward you.

keyword: non-roller wont roll won't roll locked in park free roll manual park release shift lock release
title: Non-Roller
question: What is a non-roller?
answer: A non-roller is a vehicle that cannot be confirmed movable in neutral or free-roll, or still has a parking brake, park lock, or another condition preventing safe rolling.
raw_text: Definition: A vehicle that cannot be confirmed movable in neutral or free-roll, or that still has parking brake, park lock, or another condition preventing safe rolling. Aliases: non roller, won't roll, wont roll, locked in park. Related concepts: manual park release, shift lock release, wheel skates, tire skates.

keyword: when to classify vehicle as non-roller manual park release shift-lock release wheel skates dollies
title: When to Classify a Vehicle as Non-Roller
question: When should a vehicle be treated as a non-roller?
answer: Treat the unit as a non-roller if you cannot confirm neutral or free-roll, the parking brake remains applied, required 12V power is unavailable, the key is not recognized when needed, or the manual release method is unavailable or not confirmed. Use skates, dollies, or another approved alternate handling method instead of forcing the driveline.
raw_text: If signals: cannot confirm neutral or free-roll; parking brake remains applied; no usable 12V power when required by the OEM method; no recognized key when required by the OEM method; manual release method is unavailable or not confirmed. Then action: Treat the unit as a non-roller and switch to skates, dollies, or another approved alternate handling method instead of forcing the driveline. Exception: A verified OEM manual release exists for that exact year, make, and model and also allows brake release.
